stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] A 28-year-old G1P0 client with a history of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) at 28 weeks gestation presents with new-onset hypertension and proteinuria. Based on the documentation, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions the nurse should take, and two parameters the nurse should monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: I'm calling about the 28-year-old G1P0 at 28 weeks with SLE. At 14:30, her BP is 152/98 mmHg with 3+ proteinuria.
Background: This is a high-risk pregnancy due to chronic SLE. She has been stable until this acute change.
Assessment: She has a faint malar rash, joint pain, and 2+ pitting edema. The key differential is an SLE nephritis flare versus preeclampsia with severe features.
Recommendation: I request orders for stat serum complement (C3/C4) and anti-dsDNA levels, plus a rheumatology consult to guide treatment.

tabs.title: High-Risk OB & Autoimmune Log
tabs.content:
TestResultPreeclampsia PatternSLE Flare Pattern
Serum Complement (C3/C4)Low (Critically)Normal / HighLow
Anti-dsDNAMarkedly ElevatedNegativeHigh
Uric Acid4.2 mg/dLElevated (> 6 mg/dL)Normal
Blood Pressure152/98 mmHgHighHigh

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Time: 14:30
Blood Pressure: 152/98 mmHg
Heart Rate: 92/min
Respiratory Rate: 20/min
Edema: 2+ pitting edema in lower extremities

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content:
Serum Complement (C3/C4): Low (Critically)
Anti-dsDNA: Markedly Elevated
Uric Acid: 4.2 mg/dL
Urinalysis: Protein 3+

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Appears uncomfortable, but alert and oriented. Skin: Faint malar rash noted on face. 2+ pitting edema in lower extremities bilaterally. Cardiovascular: Regular heart rate and rhythm. No murmurs auscultated. Respiratory: Clear breath sounds bilaterally. No cough or shortness of breath. Musculoskeletal: Reports joint pain in hands and knees. No visible joint swelling or redness. Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and equal bilaterally.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Renal Ultrasound: Kidneys appear normal in size and echotexture. No hydronephrosis noted. Chest X-Ray: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormalities detected. Heart size within normal limits.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Nursing Priorities: 1. Monitor blood pressure and urine protein levels frequently. 2. Administer medications as prescribed (corticosteroids). 3. Assess for signs and symptoms of worsening SLE flare or preeclampsia. 4. Provide emotional support and education to the client and her family. Nursing Interventions: 1. Monitor vital signs every 4 hours, or more frequently if indicated. 2. Obtain daily weights to assess for fluid retention. 3. Monitor fetal heart rate and activity. 4. Collaborate with the healthcare provider and rheumatology specialist regarding treatment plan.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Medications: - Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV every 12 hours - Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily - Furosemide 20 mg IV PRN for edema

parameterOptions.text: Fetal growth and umbilical artery doppler.
Presence of a positive Babinski sign.

conditions.c1: Incorrect. While symptoms overlap, the lab results (critically low C3/C4, markedly elevated anti-dsDNA, and normal uric acid) are classic for an SLE flare. Preeclampsia typically presents with normal or high complement levels and elevated uric acid.
conditions.c2: Correct. The combination of hypertension, proteinuria, malar rash, joint pain, low serum complement levels, and high anti-dsDNA titers strongly indicates an active SLE nephritis flare. This autoimmune process causes immune complex deposition in the glomeruli.
conditions.c3: Incorrect. Gestational hypertension is defined as new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks gestation without significant proteinuria. This client has 3+ proteinuria, which excludes this diagnosis.

actions.a1: Correct. Corticosteroids are the primary treatment for an SLE flare. They work by suppressing the overactive immune system, reducing inflammation and preventing further organ damage, particularly to the kidneys.
actions.a2: Correct. Serum complement levels (C3/C4) are consumed during an active SLE flare, causing them to be low. Monitoring these levels is a key indicator of treatment effectiveness; they should rise as the flare resolves.
actions.a3: Incorrect. Magnesium sulfate is for seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia with severe features. This client's presentation is due to an SLE flare, and this medication would not address the underlying autoimmune cause.
actions.a4: Incorrect. Phototherapy is used to treat neonatal hyperbilirubinemia after birth. It is irrelevant to the management of a maternal SLE flare during the antepartum period.

parameters.p1: Correct. Monitoring serum complement levels and anti-dsDNA antibody titers is essential for assessing the activity of the SLE flare and the client's response to immunosuppressive therapy. These are direct markers of the disease process.
parameters.p2: Correct. Active SLE during pregnancy significantly increases the risk of uteroplacental insufficiency, leading to fetal growth restriction and other adverse fetal outcomes. Close fetal surveillance is a critical component of care.
parameters.p3: Incorrect. While corticosteroid therapy can cause hyperglycemia, monitoring fasting glucose is not a primary parameter for the underlying SLE flare itself. It is a potential side effect to monitor, but not a priority parameter for the disease.
parameters.p4: Incorrect. A positive Babinski sign indicates an upper motor neuron lesion. While CNS lupus is a possibility, it is not suggested by the current presentation, which is primarily renal, making this an inappropriate priority parameter.

pearls.description: SLE flares during pregnancy can mimic or exacerbate preeclampsia, making diagnosis challenging. Key differentiators are lab values like complement levels and anti-dsDNA.
pearls.strategy: Always consider SLE flare in pregnant clients with known SLE presenting with hypertension and proteinuria. Check SLE-specific labs promptly.

pearls.description: Corticosteroids are a mainstay of SLE flare treatment, but can cause hyperglycemia. Monitor glucose levels, especially in pregnant clients.
pearls.strategy: Implement glucose monitoring protocols when initiating or increasing corticosteroid doses in pregnant clients with SLE.

traps.description: Misdiagnosing SLE flare as preeclampsia can lead to inappropriate treatment with magnesium sulfate, which does not address the underlying autoimmune process.
traps.strategy: Thoroughly evaluate lab results (C3/C4, anti-dsDNA, uric acid) to differentiate between SLE flare and preeclampsia before initiating treatment.

traps.description: Delaying treatment of an SLE flare can lead to irreversible kidney damage and adverse fetal outcomes.
traps.strategy: Promptly initiate corticosteroid therapy and involve a rheumatologist in the management of pregnant clients with suspected SLE flare.

mnemonics.description: Low Complement, Uric Acid Normal, Proteinuria, Ultrasound Normal Kidneys, SLE labs positive (dsDNA)
